g.data-narration: Welcome to Queue Operations! Learn how FIFO data structures work and their implementations.
A Queue follows FIFO - First In First Out. Like a ticket line: first person arrives gets served first.
g.text: A linear data structure following FIFO (First In First Out) principle.
Line of people at ticket counter

g.data-narration: Every queue has two ends: Front where elements leave, and Rear where new elements join the line.
Six key operations: enqueue adds at rear, dequeue removes from front, peek views front element.
Linear queue using arrays has a flaw: as we dequeue, front moves forward wasting precious memory space.
g.text: In linear implementation, front index keeps moving forward, wasting memory!

g.data-narration: Circular Queue solves this! Using modulo arithmetic, we wrap around to reuse freed space efficiently.
g.text: Array treated as circular! Uses modulo (%) to wrap around indices.

g.data-narration: See the magic! After dequeues, adding elements wraps around using modulo, filling previously empty slots.
Here is practical C code for circular queue operations: isFull, isEmpty, enqueue, dequeue, and peek functions.
Five main queue types: Simple, Circular, Priority, Deque, and their restricted variants for specialized use.
Queues power CPU scheduling, BFS graph traversal, I/O buffering, and message queues in distributed systems.
Stack uses LIFO (last-in-first-out) with push/pop at one end. Queue uses FIFO with enqueue/dequeue at two ends.
Always check isEmpty before dequeue! Remember: front and rear pointers, and reset when queue becomes empty.
